flashcards.back: Deadlock is a situation where a set of processes are permanently blocked because each process is holding some resource and waiting for another resource held by another process, creating a circular wait condition.

flashcards.front: What are the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
flashcards.back: 1) Mutual Exclusion: Only one process can use a resource at a time. 2) Hold and Wait: Process holds at least one resource while waiting for others. 3) No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from processes. 4) Circular Wait: A circular chain of processes exists where resource held by the each waits for a next.

flashcards.front: List the four methods for handling deadlocks.
flashcards.back: 1) Deadlock Prevention: Ensure at least one of the four conditions cannot hold. 2) Deadlock Avoidance: Use algorithms like Banker's to avoid unsafe states. 3) Deadlock Detection: Periodically check for deadlocks and identify processes involved. 4) Deadlock Recovery: Terminate processes or preempt resources to break deadlock.

flashcards.front: What is the Banker's Algorithm used for?
flashcards.back: Banker's Algorithm is a deadlock avoidance algorithm that ensures the system always remains in a safe state by checking if allocating resources to a process would lead to a safe sequence of execution for all processes.

flashcards.front: What is a safe state in deadlock avoidance?
flashcards.back: A safe state is when the system can allocate resources to all processes in some order (safe sequence) without entering deadlock. Even if all processes request maximum resources, they can still complete.

flashcards.front: What is a Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)?
flashcards.back: A directed graph that represents the allocation of resources to processes. Nodes represent processes (P) and resources (R), with edges showing request (P→R) and assignment (R→P) relationships.

flashcards.front: How does the Ostrich Algorithm handle deadlocks?
flashcards.back: The Ostrich Algorithm pretends there is no problem and does nothing to prevent, avoid, or detect deadlocks. It is used when deadlocks are rare and cost of handling them outweighs the cost of occasional system crashes.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance?
flashcards.deadlock avoidance: Prevention ensures one of the four Coffman conditions cannot hold by system design, while avoidance dynamically analyzes resource requests (like Banker's) to prevent unsafe states.

flashcards.front: What is the Wait-Die scheme in deadlock prevention?
flashcards.back: Wait-Die is a preemptive technique for deadlock prevention. When an older transaction requests a resource held by a younger transaction, it waits. When a younger transaction requests a resource held by an older one, it dies (aborts).

flashcards.front: What is the Wound-Wait scheme in deadlock prevention?
flashcards.back: Wound-Wait is a preemptive technique. When an older transaction requests a resource held by a younger one, the older one wounds (preempts) the younger one. When a younger transaction requests an older one's resource, it waits.
The detection algorithm periodically runs to detect if a set of processes is in deadlock. It constructs a wait-for graph (from RAG by removing resource nodes) and checks for cycles.

flashcards.front: What are the methods for deadlock recovery?
flashcards.back: 1) Process Termination: Abort all deadlocked processes or one at a time until deadlock is broken. 2) Resource Preemption: Forcefully take resources from processes (victim selection) and give them to others.

A wait-for graph is derived from the Resource Allocation Graph by removing resource nodes and keeping only process-to-process edges. An edge P1→P2 means P1 is waiting for a resource held by P2. A cycle indicates deadlock.

flashcards.front: How does circular wait prevention work?
flashcards.back: Circular wait is prevented by imposing a total ordering of resource types. Processes can only request resources in increasing order of their type numbers, preventing the circular wait condition.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Banker's Algorithm?
flashcards.back: Banker's Algorithm has a time complexity of O(m × n²) where m is the number of resource types and n is the number of processes, due to checking all possible safe sequences.
